----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all [[:en:Humans|Humans]], in narrower sense the entire [[:en:World population|World population]], or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''[[:en:Deutsches Wörterbuch|Deutschen Wörterbuch]]'' (von ''[[:en:Jacob Grimm|Jacob Grimm]]'' und ''[[:en:Wilhelm Grimm|Wilhelm Grimm]]'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individual [[:en:Person|human]] and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of [[:en:Person|human]]<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “[[:en:Human|human]] as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a [[:en:World|world]] of [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of [[:en:Adam|Adam]]—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the [[:en:Age of Discovery|Age of Discovery]] and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as [[:en:Race (human categorization)|race]] emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, [[:en:Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|Buffon]] and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in [[:en:Climate|climate]], [[:en:Nutrition|diet]], and [[:en:Sion, Switzerland|customs]], thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, [[:en:Voltaire|Voltaire]] argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the [[:en:19th century|19th century]], and in [[:en:1859|1859]], [[:en:Paul Broca|Broca]], who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal [[:en:Species|species]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as [[:en:Social Darwinism|social Darwinism]], which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by [[:en:Imperialism|imperialism]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between [[:en:Citizenship|citizen]] and [[:en:Barbarian|barbarian]], and between [[:en:Christians|Christian]] and [[:en:Heiden|pagan]], were fundamental; however, with the advent of the [[:en:Age of Enlightenment|Age of Enlightenment]], a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of [[:en:Human|human]], whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">[[:en:Encyclopædia Britannica|Encyclopædia Britannica]] [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the [[:en:Canine tooth|canine tooth]], and the regression of the [[:en:Tail|tail]], among others<ref name="britan" />.
== Notes ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Anthropology|Anthropology]]. [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural Anthropology]], [[:en:Social anthropology|Social Anthropology]], [[:en:Biological anthropology|Physical Anthropology]]. * [[:en:Human condition|human nature]]. * [[:en:Humanitarianism|humanitarianism]]. * {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモサピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホモ・サピエンス|1=Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15978631}}. * [[:en:Philanthropy|philanthropy]] (a term derived from “to love” + “humanity”). * [[:en:Humanism|humanism]]. * [[:en:Humanism|humanism (Renaissance)]]. * [[:en:Person|person]]、[[:en:Person|human]] (a term focusing on [[:en:Interpersonal relationship|relationship]] as used in the humanities and social sciences), [[:en:Human|human]] (in biology). * [[:en:Human evolution|human evolution]]. * [[:en:Spaceship Earth|Spaceship Earth]]. * [[:en:Cosmopolitanism|cosmopolitanism]], {{ill|global citizen|de|Weltbürger|ja|地球市民|zh|地球公民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/global citizen|1=global citizen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424571}}, {{ill|Earthling|de|Erdmensch|ja|地球人|zh|地球人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthling|1=Earthling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1349305}}. * [[:en:Humanoid|humanoid]].
== External Links == * {{Kotobank}}
== See also ==
* [[:en:Cradle of Humankind|Cradle of Humankind]] (region where hominization occurred). * [[:en:Human history|Human history]] (from earliest fossil discoveries to the present). * [[:en:Human evolution|Human evolution]] [[:en:Homo sapiens|Homo sapiens]] * [[:en:World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage]] Heritage of Humanity * [[:en:Human rights|Human rights]] (subjective rights to which everyone is equally entitled). * [[:en:Anthropocene|Anthropocene]] (youngest epoch of earth history, the "age of mankind"). * [[:en:World population|World population]] (number of people on earth)

----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all [[:en:Humans|Humans]], in narrower sense the entire [[:en:World population|World population]], or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''[[:en:Deutsches Wörterbuch|Deutschen Wörterbuch]]'' (von ''[[:en:Jacob Grimm|Jacob Grimm]]'' und ''[[:en:Wilhelm Grimm|Wilhelm Grimm]]'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individual [[:en:Person|human]] and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of [[:en:Person|human]]<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “[[:en:Human|human]] as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a [[:en:World|world]] of [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of [[:en:Adam|Adam]]—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the [[:en:Age of Discovery|Age of Discovery]] and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as [[:en:Race (human categorization)|race]] emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, [[:en:Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|Buffon]] and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in [[:en:Climate|climate]], [[:en:Nutrition|diet]], and [[:en:Sion, Switzerland|customs]], thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, [[:en:Voltaire|Voltaire]] argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the [[:en:19th century|19th century]], and in [[:en:1859|1859]], [[:en:Paul Broca|Broca]], who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal [[:en:Species|species]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as [[:en:Social Darwinism|social Darwinism]], which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by [[:en:Imperialism|imperialism]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between [[:en:Citizenship|citizen]] and [[:en:Barbarian|barbarian]], and between [[:en:Christians|Christian]] and [[:en:Heiden|pagan]], were fundamental; however, with the advent of the [[:en:Age of Enlightenment|Age of Enlightenment]], a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of [[:en:Human|human]], whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">[[:en:Encyclopædia Britannica|Encyclopædia Britannica]] [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the [[:en:Canine tooth|canine tooth]], and the regression of the [[:en:Tail|tail]], among others<ref name="britan" />.
== Notes ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Anthropology|Anthropology]]. [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural Anthropology]], [[:en:Social anthropology|Social Anthropology]], [[:en:Biological anthropology|Physical Anthropology]]. * [[:en:Human condition|human nature]]. * [[:en:Humanitarianism|humanitarianism]]. * {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモサピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホモ・サピエンス|1=Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15978631}}. * [[:en:Philanthropy|philanthropy]] (a term derived from “to love” + “humanity”). * [[:en:Humanism|humanism]]. * [[:en:Humanism|humanism (Renaissance)]]. * [[:en:Person|person]]、[[:en:Person|human]] (a term focusing on [[:en:Interpersonal relationship|relationship]] as used in the humanities and social sciences), [[:en:Human|human]] (in biology). * [[:en:Human evolution|human evolution]]. * [[:en:Spaceship Earth|Spaceship Earth]]. * [[:en:Cosmopolitanism|cosmopolitanism]], {{ill|global citizen|de|Weltbürger|ja|地球市民|zh|地球公民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/global citizen|1=global citizen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424571}}, {{ill|Earthling|de|Erdmensch|ja|地球人|zh|地球人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthling|1=Earthling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1349305}}. * [[:en:Humanoid|humanoid]].
== External Links == * {{Kotobank}}
== See also ==
* [[:en:Cradle of Humankind|Cradle of Humankind]] (region where hominization occurred). * [[:en:Human history|Human history]] (from earliest fossil discoveries to the present). * [[:en:Human evolution|Human evolution]] [[:en:Homo sapiens|Homo sapiens]] * [[:en:World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage]] Heritage of Humanity * [[:en:Human rights|Human rights]] (subjective rights to which everyone is equally entitled). * [[:en:Anthropocene|Anthropocene]] (youngest epoch of earth history, the "age of mankind"). * [[:en:World population|World population]] (number of people on earth)

----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all [[:en:Humans|Humans]], in narrower sense the entire [[:en:World population|World population]], or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''[[:en:Deutsches Wörterbuch|Deutschen Wörterbuch]]'' (von ''[[:en:Jacob Grimm|Jacob Grimm]]'' und ''[[:en:Wilhelm Grimm|Wilhelm Grimm]]'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individual [[:en:Person|human]] and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of [[:en:Person|human]]<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “[[:en:Human|human]] as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a [[:en:World|world]] of [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of [[:en:Adam|Adam]]—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the [[:en:Age of Discovery|Age of Discovery]] and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as [[:en:Race (human categorization)|race]] emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, [[:en:Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|Buffon]] and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in [[:en:Climate|climate]], [[:en:Nutrition|diet]], and [[:en:Sion, Switzerland|customs]], thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, [[:en:Voltaire|Voltaire]] argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the [[:en:19th century|19th century]], and in [[:en:1859|1859]], [[:en:Paul Broca|Broca]], who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal [[:en:Species|species]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as [[:en:Social Darwinism|social Darwinism]], which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by [[:en:Imperialism|imperialism]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between [[:en:Citizenship|citizen]] and [[:en:Barbarian|barbarian]], and between [[:en:Christians|Christian]] and [[:en:Heiden|pagan]], were fundamental; however, with the advent of the [[:en:Age of Enlightenment|Age of Enlightenment]], a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of [[:en:Human|human]], whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">[[:en:Encyclopædia Britannica|Encyclopædia Britannica]] [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the [[:en:Canine tooth|canine tooth]], and the regression of the [[:en:Tail|tail]], among others<ref name="britan" />.
== Notes ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Anthropology|Anthropology]]. [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural Anthropology]], [[:en:Social anthropology|Social Anthropology]], [[:en:Biological anthropology|Physical Anthropology]]. * [[:en:Human condition|human nature]]. * [[:en:Humanitarianism|humanitarianism]]. * {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモサピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホモ・サピエンス|1=Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15978631}}. * [[:en:Philanthropy|philanthropy]] (a term derived from “to love” + “humanity”). * [[:en:Humanism|humanism]]. * [[:en:Humanism|humanism (Renaissance)]]. * [[:en:Person|person]]、[[:en:Person|human]] (a term focusing on [[:en:Interpersonal relationship|relationship]] as used in the humanities and social sciences), [[:en:Human|human]] (in biology). * [[:en:Human evolution|human evolution]]. * [[:en:Spaceship Earth|Spaceship Earth]]. * [[:en:Cosmopolitanism|cosmopolitanism]], {{ill|global citizen|de|Weltbürger|ja|地球市民|zh|地球公民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/global citizen|1=global citizen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424571}}, {{ill|Earthling|de|Erdmensch|ja|地球人|zh|地球人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthling|1=Earthling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1349305}}. * [[:en:Humanoid|humanoid]].
== External Links == * {{Kotobank}}
== See also ==
* [[:en:Cradle of Humankind|Cradle of Humankind]] (region where hominization occurred). * [[:en:Human history|Human history]] (from earliest fossil discoveries to the present). * [[:en:Human evolution|Human evolution]] [[:en:Homo sapiens|Homo sapiens]] * [[:en:World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage]] Heritage of Humanity * [[:en:Human rights|Human rights]] (subjective rights to which everyone is equally entitled). * [[:en:Anthropocene|Anthropocene]] (youngest epoch of earth history, the "age of mankind"). * [[:en:World population|World population]] (number of people on earth)

----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all [[:en:Humans|Humans]], in narrower sense the entire [[:en:World population|World population]], or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''[[:en:Deutsches Wörterbuch|Deutschen Wörterbuch]]'' (von ''[[:en:Jacob Grimm|Jacob Grimm]]'' und ''[[:en:Wilhelm Grimm|Wilhelm Grimm]]'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individual [[:en:Person|human]] and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of [[:en:Person|human]]<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “[[:en:Human|human]] as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a [[:en:World|world]] of [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of [[:en:Adam|Adam]]—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the [[:en:Age of Discovery|Age of Discovery]] and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as [[:en:Race (human categorization)|race]] emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, [[:en:Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|Buffon]] and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in [[:en:Climate|climate]], [[:en:Nutrition|diet]], and [[:en:Sion, Switzerland|customs]], thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, [[:en:Voltaire|Voltaire]] argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the [[:en:19th century|19th century]], and in [[:en:1859|1859]], [[:en:Paul Broca|Broca]], who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal [[:en:Species|species]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as [[:en:Social Darwinism|social Darwinism]], which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by [[:en:Imperialism|imperialism]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between [[:en:Citizenship|citizen]] and [[:en:Barbarian|barbarian]], and between [[:en:Christians|Christian]] and [[:en:Heiden|pagan]], were fundamental; however, with the advent of the [[:en:Age of Enlightenment|Age of Enlightenment]], a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of [[:en:Human|human]], whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">[[:en:Encyclopædia Britannica|Encyclopædia Britannica]] [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the [[:en:Canine tooth|canine tooth]], and the regression of the [[:en:Tail|tail]], among others<ref name="britan" />.

[[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural Anthropology]], [[:en:Social anthropology|Social Anthropology]], [[:en:Biological anthropology|Physical Anthropology]]. * [[:en:Human condition|human nature]]. * [[:en:Humanitarianism|humanitarianism]]. * {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモサピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホモ・サピエンス|1=Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15978631}}. * [[:en:Philanthropy|philanthropy]] (a term derived from “to love” + “humanity”). * [[:en:Humanism|humanism]]. * [[:en:Humanism|humanism (Renaissance)]]. * [[:en:Person|person]]、[[:en:Person|human]] (a term focusing on [[:en:Interpersonal relationship|relationship]] as used in the humanities and social sciences), [[:en:Human|human]] (in biology). * [[:en:Human evolution|human evolution]]. * [[:en:Spaceship Earth|Spaceship Earth]]. * [[:en:Cosmopolitanism|cosmopolitanism]], {{ill|global citizen|de|Weltbürger|ja|地球市民|zh|地球公民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/global citizen|1=global citizen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424571}}, {{ill|Earthling|de|Erdmensch|ja|地球人|zh|地球人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthling|1=Earthling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1349305}}. * [[:en:Humanoid|humanoid]].

----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all [[:en:Humans|Humans]], in narrower sense the entire [[:en:World population|World population]], or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''[[:en:Deutsches Wörterbuch|Deutschen Wörterbuch]]'' (von ''[[:en:Jacob Grimm|Jacob Grimm]]'' und ''[[:en:Wilhelm Grimm|Wilhelm Grimm]]'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individual [[:en:Person|human]] and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of [[:en:Person|human]]<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “[[:en:Human|human]] as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of [[:en:Person|human]] as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a [[:en:World|world]] of [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of [[:en:Adam|Adam]]—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the [[:en:Age of Discovery|Age of Discovery]] and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as [[:en:Race (human categorization)|race]] emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, [[:en:Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|Buffon]] and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in [[:en:Climate|climate]], [[:en:Nutrition|diet]], and [[:en:Sion, Switzerland|customs]], thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, [[:en:Voltaire|Voltaire]] argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the [[:en:19th century|19th century]], and in [[:en:1859|1859]], [[:en:Paul Broca|Broca]], who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal [[:en:Species|species]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as [[:en:Social Darwinism|social Darwinism]], which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by [[:en:Imperialism|imperialism]]<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between [[:en:Citizenship|citizen]] and [[:en:Barbarian|barbarian]], and between [[:en:Christians|Christian]] and [[:en:Heiden|pagan]], were fundamental; however, with the advent of the [[:en:Age of Enlightenment|Age of Enlightenment]], a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of [[:en:Human|human]], whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">[[:en:Encyclopædia Britannica|Encyclopædia Britannica]] [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the [[:en:Canine tooth|canine tooth]], and the regression of the [[:en:Tail|tail]], among others<ref name="britan" />.
== Notes ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Anthropology|Anthropology]]. [[:en:Cultural anthropology|Cultural Anthropology]], [[:en:Social anthropology|Social Anthropology]], [[:en:Biological anthropology|Physical Anthropology]]. * [[:en:Human condition|human nature]]. * [[:en:Humanitarianism|humanitarianism]]. * {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモサピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホモ・サピエンス|1=Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15978631}}. * [[:en:Philanthropy|philanthropy]] (a term derived from “to love” + “humanity”). * [[:en:Humanism|humanism]]. * [[:en:Humanism|humanism (Renaissance)]]. * [[:en:Person|person]]、[[:en:Person|human]] (a term focusing on [[:en:Interpersonal relationship|relationship]] as used in the humanities and social sciences), [[:en:Human|human]] (in biology). * [[:en:Human evolution|human evolution]]. * [[:en:Spaceship Earth|Spaceship Earth]]. * [[:en:Cosmopolitanism|cosmopolitanism]], {{ill|global citizen|de|Weltbürger|ja|地球市民|zh|地球公民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/global citizen|1=global citizen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424571}}, {{ill|Earthling|de|Erdmensch|ja|地球人|zh|地球人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthling|1=Earthling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1349305}}. * [[:en:Humanoid|humanoid]].

----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all {{ill|Humans|en|Humans|WD=Q16334295|lt=Humans}}, in narrower sense the entire {{ill|World population|en|World population|WD=Q11188|lt=World population}}, or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''{{ill|Deutsches Wörterbuch|en|Deutsches Wörterbuch|WD=Q96321|lt=Deutschen Wörterbuch}}'' (von ''{{ill|Jacob Grimm|en|Jacob Grimm|WD=Q6701|lt=Jacob Grimm}}'' und ''{{ill|Wilhelm Grimm|en|Wilhelm Grimm|WD=Q6714|lt=Wilhelm Grimm}}'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individual {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}}<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “{{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}} as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a {{ill|World|en|World|WD=Q16502|lt=world}} of {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity}}, in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of {{ill|Adam|en|Adam|WD=Q70899|lt=Adam}}—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the {{ill|Age of Discovery|en|Age of Discovery|WD=Q133641|lt=Age of Discovery}} and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as {{ill|Race (human categorization)|en|Race (human categorization)|WD=Q3254959|lt=race}} emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, {{ill|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|en|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|WD=Q229264|lt=Buffon}} and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in {{ill|Climate|en|Climate|WD=Q7937|lt=climate}}, {{ill|Nutrition|en|Nutrition|WD=Q2138622|lt=diet}}, and {{ill|Sion, Switzerland|en|Sion, Switzerland|WD=Q68136|lt=customs}}, thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, {{ill|Voltaire|en|Voltaire|WD=Q9068|lt=Voltaire}} argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the {{ill|19th century|en|19th century|WD=Q6955|lt=19th century}}, and in {{ill|1859|en|1859|WD=Q7687|lt=1859}}, {{ill|Paul Broca|en|Paul Broca|WD=Q311276|lt=Broca}}, who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal {{ill|Species|en|Species|WD=Q7432|lt=species}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as {{ill|Social Darwinism|en|Social Darwinism|WD=Q202261|lt=social Darwinism}}, which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by {{ill|Imperialism|en|Imperialism|WD=Q7260|lt=imperialism}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between {{ill|Citizenship|en|Citizenship|WD=Q42138|lt=citizen}} and {{ill|Barbarian|en|Barbarian|WD=Q134313|lt=barbarian}}, and between {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|WD=Q106039|lt=Christian}} and {{ill|Heiden|en|Heiden|WD=Q252034|lt=pagan}}, were fundamental; however, with the advent of the {{ill|Age of Enlightenment|en|Age of Enlightenment|WD=Q12539|lt=Age of Enlightenment}}, a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}}, whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">{{ill|Encyclopædia Britannica|en|Encyclopædia Britannica|WD=Q455|lt=Encyclopædia Britannica}} [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the {{ill|Canine tooth|en|Canine tooth|WD=Q19625|lt=canine tooth}}, and the regression of the {{ill|Tail|en|Tail|WD=Q60960|lt=tail}}, among others<ref name="britan" />.
== Notes ==

* {{ill|Cradle of Humankind|en|Cradle of Humankind|WD=Q1130112|lt=Cradle of Humankind}} (region where hominization occurred). * {{ill|Human history|en|Human history|WD=Q200325|lt=Human history}} (from earliest fossil discoveries to the present). * {{ill|Human evolution|en|Human evolution|WD=Q83944|lt=Human evolution}} {{ill|Homo sapiens|en|Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631|lt=Homo sapiens}} * {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Heritage}} Heritage of Humanity * {{ill|Human rights|en|Human rights|WD=Q8458|lt=Human rights}} (subjective rights to which everyone is equally entitled). * {{ill|Anthropocene|en|Anthropocene|WD=Q26841|lt=Anthropocene}} (youngest epoch of earth history, the "age of mankind"). * {{ill|World population|en|World population|WD=Q11188|lt=World population}} (number of people on earth)

This term encompasses both the aspect of “{{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}} as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a {{ill|World|en|World|WD=Q16502|lt=world}} of {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity}}, in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of {{ill|Adam|en|Adam|WD=Q70899|lt=Adam}}—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the {{ill|Age of Discovery|en|Age of Discovery|WD=Q133641|lt=Age of Discovery}} and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as {{ill|Race (human categorization)|en|Race (human categorization)|WD=Q3254959|lt=race}} emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, {{ill|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|en|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|WD=Q229264|lt=Buffon}} and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in {{ill|Climate|en|Climate|WD=Q7937|lt=climate}}, {{ill|Nutrition|en|Nutrition|WD=Q2138622|lt=diet}}, and {{ill|Sion, Switzerland|en|Sion, Switzerland|WD=Q68136|lt=customs}}, thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, {{ill|Voltaire|en|Voltaire|WD=Q9068|lt=Voltaire}} argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the {{ill|19th century|en|19th century|WD=Q6955|lt=19th century}}, and in {{ill|1859|en|1859|WD=Q7687|lt=1859}}, {{ill|Paul Broca|en|Paul Broca|WD=Q311276|lt=Broca}}, who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal {{ill|Species|en|Species|WD=Q7432|lt=species}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as {{ill|Social Darwinism|en|Social Darwinism|WD=Q202261|lt=social Darwinism}}, which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by {{ill|Imperialism|en|Imperialism|WD=Q7260|lt=imperialism}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between {{ill|Citizenship|en|Citizenship|WD=Q42138|lt=citizen}} and {{ill|Barbarian|en|Barbarian|WD=Q134313|lt=barbarian}}, and between {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|WD=Q106039|lt=Christian}} and {{ill|Heiden|en|Heiden|WD=Q252034|lt=pagan}}, were fundamental; however, with the advent of the {{ill|Age of Enlightenment|en|Age of Enlightenment|WD=Q12539|lt=Age of Enlightenment}}, a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}}, whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">{{ill|Encyclopædia Britannica|en|Encyclopædia Britannica|WD=Q455|lt=Encyclopædia Britannica}} [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the {{ill|Canine tooth|en|Canine tooth|WD=Q19625|lt=canine tooth}}, and the regression of the {{ill|Tail|en|Tail|WD=Q60960|lt=tail}}, among others<ref name="britan" />.

{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all {{ill|Humans|en|Humans|WD=Q16334295|lt=Humans}}, in narrower sense the entire {{ill|World population|en|World population|WD=Q11188|lt=World population}}, or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''{{ill|Deutsches Wörterbuch|en|Deutsches Wörterbuch|WD=Q96321|lt=Deutschen Wörterbuch}}'' (von ''{{ill|Jacob Grimm|en|Jacob Grimm|WD=Q6701|lt=Jacob Grimm}}'' und ''{{ill|Wilhelm Grimm|en|Wilhelm Grimm|WD=Q6714|lt=Wilhelm Grimm}}'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individual {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}}<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “{{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}} as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a {{ill|World|en|World|WD=Q16502|lt=world}} of {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity}}, in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of {{ill|Adam|en|Adam|WD=Q70899|lt=Adam}}—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the {{ill|Age of Discovery|en|Age of Discovery|WD=Q133641|lt=Age of Discovery}} and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as {{ill|Race (human categorization)|en|Race (human categorization)|WD=Q3254959|lt=race}} emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, {{ill|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|en|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|WD=Q229264|lt=Buffon}} and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in {{ill|Climate|en|Climate|WD=Q7937|lt=climate}}, {{ill|Nutrition|en|Nutrition|WD=Q2138622|lt=diet}}, and {{ill|Sion, Switzerland|en|Sion, Switzerland|WD=Q68136|lt=customs}}, thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, {{ill|Voltaire|en|Voltaire|WD=Q9068|lt=Voltaire}} argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the {{ill|19th century|en|19th century|WD=Q6955|lt=19th century}}, and in {{ill|1859|en|1859|WD=Q7687|lt=1859}}, {{ill|Paul Broca|en|Paul Broca|WD=Q311276|lt=Broca}}, who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal {{ill|Species|en|Species|WD=Q7432|lt=species}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as {{ill|Social Darwinism|en|Social Darwinism|WD=Q202261|lt=social Darwinism}}, which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by {{ill|Imperialism|en|Imperialism|WD=Q7260|lt=imperialism}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between {{ill|Citizenship|en|Citizenship|WD=Q42138|lt=citizen}} and {{ill|Barbarian|en|Barbarian|WD=Q134313|lt=barbarian}}, and between {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|WD=Q106039|lt=Christian}} and {{ill|Heiden|en|Heiden|WD=Q252034|lt=pagan}}, were fundamental; however, with the advent of the {{ill|Age of Enlightenment|en|Age of Enlightenment|WD=Q12539|lt=Age of Enlightenment}}, a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}}, whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">{{ill|Encyclopædia Britannica|en|Encyclopædia Britannica|WD=Q455|lt=Encyclopædia Britannica}} [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the {{ill|Canine tooth|en|Canine tooth|WD=Q19625|lt=canine tooth}}, and the regression of the {{ill|Tail|en|Tail|WD=Q60960|lt=tail}}, among others<ref name="britan" />.

----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=}} refers to the totality of all {{ill|Humans|en|Humans|WD=Q16334295|lt=Humans}}, in narrower sense the entire {{ill|World population|en|World population|WD=Q11188|lt=World population}}, or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''{{ill|Deutsches Wörterbuch|en|Deutsches Wörterbuch|WD=Q96321|lt=Deutschen Wörterbuch}}'' (von ''{{ill|Jacob Grimm|en|Jacob Grimm|WD=Q6701|lt=Jacob Grimm}}'' und ''{{ill|Wilhelm Grimm|en|Wilhelm Grimm|WD=Q6714|lt=Wilhelm Grimm}}'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individual {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}}<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “{{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}} as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=kind|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=European world|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a {{ill|World|en|World|WD=Q16502|lt=world}} of {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity}}, in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of {{ill|Adam|en|Adam|WD=Q70899|lt=Adam}}—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the {{ill|Age of Discovery|en|Age of Discovery|WD=Q133641|lt=Age of Discovery}} and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as {{ill|Race (human categorization)|en|Race (human categorization)|WD=Q3254959|lt=race}} emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, {{ill|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|en|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|WD=Q229264|lt=Buffon}} and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in {{ill|Climate|en|Climate|WD=Q7937|lt=climate}}, {{ill|Nutrition|en|Nutrition|WD=Q2138622|lt=diet}}, and {{ill|Sion, Switzerland|en|Sion, Switzerland|WD=Q68136|lt=customs}}, thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, {{ill|Voltaire|en|Voltaire|WD=Q9068|lt=Voltaire}} argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the {{ill|19th century|en|19th century|WD=Q6955|lt=19th century}}, and in {{ill|1859|en|1859|WD=Q7687|lt=1859}}, {{ill|Paul Broca|en|Paul Broca|WD=Q311276|lt=Broca}}, who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal {{ill|Species|en|Species|WD=Q7432|lt=species}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as {{ill|Social Darwinism|en|Social Darwinism|WD=Q202261|lt=social Darwinism}}, which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by {{ill|Imperialism|en|Imperialism|WD=Q7260|lt=imperialism}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between {{ill|Citizenship|en|Citizenship|WD=Q42138|lt=citizen}} and {{ill|Barbarian|en|Barbarian|WD=Q134313|lt=barbarian}}, and between {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|WD=Q106039|lt=Christian}} and {{ill|Heiden|en|Heiden|WD=Q252034|lt=pagan}}, were fundamental; however, with the advent of the {{ill|Age of Enlightenment|en|Age of Enlightenment|WD=Q12539|lt=Age of Enlightenment}}, a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}}, whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">{{ill|Encyclopædia Britannica|en|Encyclopædia Britannica|WD=Q455|lt=Encyclopædia Britannica}} [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the {{ill|Canine tooth|en|Canine tooth|WD=Q19625|lt=canine tooth}}, and the regression of the {{ill|Tail|en|Tail|WD=Q60960|lt=tail}}, among others<ref name="britan" />.
== Notes ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Anthropology|en|Anthropology|WD=Q23404|lt=Anthropology}}. {{ill|Cultural anthropology|en|Cultural anthropology|WD=Q28598|lt=Cultural Anthropology}}, {{ill|Social anthropology|en|Social anthropology|WD=Q29051|lt=Social Anthropology}}, {{ill|Biological anthropology|en|Biological anthropology|WD=Q27172|lt=Physical Anthropology}}. * {{ill|Human condition|en|Human condition|WD=Q1124515|lt=human nature}}. * {{ill|Humanitarianism|en|Humanitarianism|WD=Q775858|lt=humanitarianism}}. * {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモサピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホモ・サピエンス|1=Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15978631}}. * {{ill|Philanthropy|en|Philanthropy|WD=Q185733|lt=philanthropy}} (a term derived from “to love” + “humanity”). * {{ill|Humanism|en|Humanism|WD=Q46158|lt=humanism}}. * {{ill|Humanism|en|Humanism|WD=Q46158|lt=humanism (Renaissance)}}. * {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=person}}、{{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human}} (a term focusing on {{ill|Interpersonal relationship|en|Interpersonal relationship|WD=Q223642|lt=relationship}} as used in the humanities and social sciences), {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human}} (in biology). * {{ill|Human evolution|en|Human evolution|WD=Q83944|lt=human evolution}}. * {{ill|Spaceship Earth|en|Spaceship Earth|WD=Q2778319|lt=Spaceship Earth}}. * {{ill|Cosmopolitanism|en|Cosmopolitanism|WD=Q188843|lt=cosmopolitanism}}, {{ill|global citizen|de|Weltbürger|ja|地球市民|zh|地球公民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/global citizen|1=global citizen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424571}}, {{ill|Earthling|de|Erdmensch|ja|地球人|zh|地球人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earthling|1=Earthling|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1349305}}. * {{ill|Humanoid|en|Humanoid|WD=Q502931|lt=humanoid}}.
== External Links == * {{Kotobank}}
== See also ==
* {{ill|Cradle of Humankind|en|Cradle of Humankind|WD=Q1130112|lt=Cradle of Humankind}} (region where hominization occurred). * {{ill|Human history|en|Human history|WD=Q200325|lt=Human history}} (from earliest fossil discoveries to the present). * {{ill|Human evolution|en|Human evolution|WD=Q83944|lt=Human evolution}} {{ill|Homo sapiens|en|Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631|lt=Homo sapiens}} * {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Heritage}} Heritage of Humanity * {{ill|Human rights|en|Human rights|WD=Q8458|lt=Human rights}} (subjective rights to which everyone is equally entitled). * {{ill|Anthropocene|en|Anthropocene|WD=Q26841|lt=Anthropocene}} (youngest epoch of earth history, the "age of mankind"). * {{ill|World population|en|World population|WD=Q11188|lt=World population}} (number of people on earth)

----{{Draft topics|history|society|stem}} {{AfC topic|other}}
{{Draft article|subject=society}} {{ill|Mankind|de|Menschheit|WD=Q1156970|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mankind|qq=|1=Mankind}} refers to the totality of all {{ill|Humans|en|Humans|WD=Q16334295|lt=Humans|1=Humans}}, in narrower sense the entire {{ill|World population|en|World population|WD=Q11188|lt=World population|1=World population}}, or in a more abstract sense the social unity of all people, for example in the case of presumed or actual common decisions or interests of all people.<ref>[//woerterbuchnetz.de/?sigle=DWB&lemma=Menschheit Menschheit] – Eintrag im ''{{ill|Deutsches Wörterbuch|en|Deutsches Wörterbuch|WD=Q96321|lt=Deutschen Wörterbuch|1=Deutsches Wörterbuch}}'' (von ''{{ill|Jacob Grimm|en|Jacob Grimm|WD=Q6701|lt=Jacob Grimm|1=Jacob Grimm}}'' und ''{{ill|Wilhelm Grimm|en|Wilhelm Grimm|WD=Q6714|lt=Wilhelm Grimm|1=Wilhelm Grimm}}'', im ''[[Wörterbuchnetz]]''); ebenda (noch in [[:en:Brüder Grimm|ihrer]] bevorzugten [[Kleinschreibung]]) unter anderem mit: „art und gesamtheit der menschen“</ref>
==Overview== '''Jinrui''' (人類)
Humanity refers to {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human|1=Person}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individual {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human|1=Person}} and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, i.e., as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=Q11665175|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human|1=Person}}<ref name="philo_sakagami">『岩波 哲学思想事典』 p.858 [Humanity] {{ill|Takashi Sakagami|de|Takashi Sakagami|ja|阪上孝|zh|阪上孝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Sakagami|1=Takashi Sakagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11656604}} (written)</ref>. This term encompasses both the aspect of “{{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human|1=Human}} as a biological species” and that of a communality which ought to be realized as a single kind<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
== Overview == Humanity refers to the concept of {{ill|Person|en|Person|WD=Q215627|lt=human|1=Person}} as a kind that transcends the differences among individuals and {{ill|ethnic group|de|Volksgruppe|ja|民族|zh|民族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ethnic group|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Ethnic group|WD=Q41710}}, that is, as a type of {{ill|kind|de|Gattung|ja|類|zh|类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kind|1=Q11665175|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665175}} of human.
Theories concerning humanity in Western thought have undergone various transformations and debates over time, with significant theories including, for example, the monogenic theory, the polygenic theory, and the concept of humanity<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Monogenic and Polygenic Theories === (Although the {{ill|European world|de|Europäische Welt|ja|ヨーロッパ世界|zh|欧洲世界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/European world|1=Q11346242|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11346242}} was a {{ill|World|en|World|WD=Q16502|lt=world|1=World}} of {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|WD=Q5043|lt=Christianity|1=Christianity}}, in the Christian world the monogenic theory—which holds that all humanity are descendants of {{ill|Adam|en|Adam|WD=Q70899|lt=Adam|1=Adam}}—and the view of humans as bearing the image of God continued to hold persuasive power<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. However, as Europe entered the {{ill|Age of Discovery|en|Age of Discovery|WD=Q133641|lt=Age of Discovery|1=Age of Discovery}} and came into contact with people beyond its borders—with the accompanying influx of information—the question of how to regard those who appeared as “variants of human” or as {{ill|Race (human categorization)|en|Race (human categorization)|WD=Q3254959|lt=race|1=Race (human categorization)}} emerged as a major issue among Westerners<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. Regarding these issues, {{ill|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|en|Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|WD=Q229264|lt=Buffon|1=Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon}} and many others adopted the monogenic theory, arguing that differences among human groups arose from differences in {{ill|Climate|en|Climate|WD=Q7937|lt=climate|1=Climate}}, {{ill|Nutrition|en|Nutrition|WD=Q2138622|lt=diet|1=Nutrition}}, and {{ill|Sion, Switzerland|en|Sion, Switzerland|WD=Q68136|lt=customs|1=Sion, Switzerland}}, thereby asserting that humanity is a single species; in contrast, {{ill|Voltaire|en|Voltaire|WD=Q9068|lt=Voltaire|1=Voltaire}} argued for the polygenic theory<ref name="philo_sakagami" />. The conflict between the monogenic and polygenic theories persisted into the {{ill|19th century|en|19th century|WD=Q6955|lt=19th century|1=19th century}}, and in {{ill|1859|en|1859|WD=Q7687|lt=1859|1=1859}}, {{ill|Paul Broca|en|Paul Broca|WD=Q311276|lt=Broca|1=Paul Broca}}, who founded the Paris Anthropological Society, also addressed these issues—stating that the differences between races are greater than those between animal {{ill|Species|en|Species|WD=Q7432|lt=species|1=Species}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
Subsequently, concepts such as {{ill|Social Darwinism|en|Social Darwinism|WD=Q202261|lt=social Darwinism|1=Social Darwinism}}, which posited that “evolved races dominate primitive ones,” emerged, and such ideas came to be conveniently exploited by {{ill|Imperialism|en|Imperialism|WD=Q7260|lt=imperialism|1=Imperialism}}<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== The Concept of Humanity and Communality === In European thought, distinctions such as that between {{ill|Citizenship|en|Citizenship|WD=Q42138|lt=citizen|1=Citizenship}} and {{ill|Barbarian|en|Barbarian|WD=Q134313|lt=barbarian|1=Barbarian}}, and between {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|WD=Q106039|lt=Christian|1=Christians}} and {{ill|Heiden|en|Heiden|WD=Q252034|lt=pagan|1=Heiden}}, were fundamental; however, with the advent of the {{ill|Age of Enlightenment|en|Age of Enlightenment|WD=Q12539|lt=Age of Enlightenment|1=Age of Enlightenment}}, a concept of “humanity” emerged that transcended these discriminations, and the history of humanity began to be presented as a history of progress through the self-completion of human beings. In this context, the concept of humanity came to be strongly associated with a communality that was to be realized<ref name="philo_sakagami" />.
=== Humanity as a Biological Species === As a biological species, “humanity” is a collective term encompassing all entities included in the concept of {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=human|1=Human}}, whether they are extant species (those still living) or fossil species (those that exist only as fossils)<ref name="britan">{{ill|Encyclopædia Britannica|en|Encyclopædia Britannica|WD=Q455|lt=Encyclopædia Britannica|1=Encyclopædia Britannica}} [Humanity]</ref>; however, there is considerable debate regarding the scope of this inclusion, and even fossil species other than {{ill|Homo sapiens|de|Homo sapiens|ja|ホモ・サピエンス|zh|智人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homo sapiens|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Homo sapiens|WD=Q15978631}} continue to be the subject of ongoing discussion<ref name="britan" />. Common characteristics include the ability to {{ill|bipedalism|de|Bipedalismus|ja|直立歩行|zh|直立行走|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bipedalism|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 直立二足歩行|1=Erect bipedalism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22131171}}, a reduction in the size of the {{ill|Canine tooth|en|Canine tooth|WD=Q19625|lt=canine tooth|1=Canine tooth}}, and the regression of the {{ill|Tail|en|Tail|WD=Q60960|lt=tail|1=Tail}}, among others<ref name="britan" />.
